perturbation.original_text: "Confidential Information " means any information of a confidential nature relating to the Disclosing Party or a member of its Group disclosed (whether before or after the date of this Agreement and whether in writing, verbally or by any other means and whether directly or indirectly) by the Disclosing Party or by another person on behalf of the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party or to another person on behal f of the Receiving Party including, without limitation, any information relating to the Purpose or to the Disclosing Party's products, operations, processes, plans or intentions, product information, know how, design rights, trade secrets, market opportuni ties and business affairs;
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information " means any information of any nature relating to the Disclosing Party or a member of its Group disclosed (whether before or after the date of this Agreement and whether in writing, verbally or by any other means and whether directly or indirectly) by the Disclosing Party or by another person on behalf of the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party or to another person on behal f of the Receiving Party including, without limitation, any information relating to the Purpose or to the Disclosing Party's products, operations, processes, plans or intentions, product information, know how, design rights, trade secrets, market opportuni ties and business affairs;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "of a confidential nature", all information disclosed, even publicly available information, could be considered confidential under this agreement. This broadens the definition beyond what is legally recognized and could lead to disputes.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1:1 sub c Wet bescherming bedrijfsgeheimen (Wbb)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act in the Netherlands defines a trade secret as information that is secret, has commercial value because it is secret, and has been subject to reasonable steps to keep it secret. By removing the 'confidential nature' requirement, the contract attempts to protect information that is not actually a trade secret under Dutch law, thus contradicting the law's scope and intent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines "Confidential Information" as information "of a confidential nature." The changed text removes this qualifier, broadening the definition to include "any information." According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts the Trade Secrets Act in the Netherlands, which requires that information be secret to qualify as a trade secret. By removing the 'confidential nature' requirement, the contract attempts to protect information that might not meet the legal definition of a trade secret, leading to a contradiction. While the scraped snippets are not useful, the Law Explanation makes a compelling case for the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those which it applies to its own confidential information and which it warrants as providing adequate protection against any unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise security measures that are commercially reasonable, but not necessarily the same as those applied to its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the standard of care required for protecting confidential information. By stating that the measures don't need to be the same as those used for its own confidential information, it provides less protection than is generally required to maintain the confidentiality of trade secrets and could jeopardize legal protections.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1:1 sub c Wet bescherming bedrijfsgeheimen (Wbb)
perturbation.law_explanation: Dutch Trade Secrets Act requires adequate measures to protect confidential information to be considered a trade secret. By allowing for 'commercially reasonable' but potentially lesser security measures, the contract may fail to meet the legal standard for trade secret protection, contradicting the intent of the law to protect legitimately confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the protection of confidential information. The original text requires a standard of care no less than what the party uses for its own confidential information, implicitly suggesting a high standard. The changed text only requires 'commercially reasonable' measures, which may be less stringent. This contradicts the intent of the Dutch Trade Secrets Act, as explained by the LLM, which necessitates adequate measures for trade secret protection. While specific legal snippets are absent, the rationale provided by the LLM, along with the explanation of the change, supports that the modified text potentially fails to meet the legal standard for trade secret protection by allowing for lesser security measures, therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement; 5.2 which the Receiving Party can show by its written or other records was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.3 which the Receiving Party obtains or has avai lable from a source other than the Disclosing Party without breaching any obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person without an obligation of confiden tiality .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement; 5.2 which the Receiving Party believes was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party; 5.3 which the Receiving Party may obtain or has available from any source other than the Disclosing Party ; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement that the Receiving Party has to show written proof or other records that the information was in their possession prior to receipt and removes the breaching obligations. This broadens the exceptions to confidential information beyond what is legally recognized as prior knowledge.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1:1 sub c Wet bescherming bedrijfsgeheimen (Wbb)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Dutch Trade Secrets Act, information already possessed must be legitimately held. Changing the phrase to only a 'belief' eliminates the objective standard of prior possession, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to claim prior possession without evidence, therefore, it contradicts that law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Internetrecht en technologie uitgelegd door Arnoud Engelfriet (iusmentis.com) Homepage | Categorieën | Lijst A-Z | Willekeurig artikel | Herpubliceren? | Over deze site | Blog | Contact Welkom bij iusmentis.com Internetrecht: Actualiteiten en commentaar De laatste berichten van de Iusmentis blog KPN blokkeert iPhone bij wanbetaling via MDM, mag dat? ier jaar handel ik in iPhones. Ik koop deze o.a. in via Marktplaats en »» u u »» f f »» »» v v »» »» v v »» n n »» a a »» b b »» Lees alle berichten op mijn blog. Met mijn nieuwsbrief krijg je de laatste blogs wekelijks in je mailbox. De wet uitgelegd aan techneuten, en techniek aan juristen. Ius mentis biedt meer dan 350 artikelen over internetrecht en intellectueel eigendom, variërend van regels rond bloggen tot computercriminaliteit . Een uitgebreide introductie is het artikel Mag dat op Internet . De auteur en beheerder is ICT-jurist Arnoud Engelfriet ( blog , contact ). Populairste artikelen De thuiskopie in het Nederlands auteursrecht Een kopie voor strikt eigen gebruik (een "thuiskopie") mag je eigenlijk altijd maken. Echter als het werk van een illegale bron afkomstig is, dan geldt deze regeling niet. Zo'n download is in strijd met het downloadverbod. »» Beschermen van een idee - is vastleggen genoeg? Een idee of concept beschermen is lastig. Octrooi- en auteurswetten eisen een behoorlijke mate van uitwerking of concretisering voordat een concept beschermd kan worden. Het idee opschrijven en ergens deponeren ('vastleggen') biedt meestal maar zeer weinig bescherming. »» Algemene voorwaarden Algemene voorwaarden zijn voorwaarden die bij meerdere overeenkomsten worden gebruikt. Ze zijn ook bindend als de wederpartij ze niet gelezen heeft, maar ze mogen niet onredelijk bezwarend zijn. »» Colportage op internet: de Wet Koop op Afstand De wet Koop op Afstand beschermt de consument door bindende regels bij koop via Internet. De belangrijkste zijn het recht op ontbinding van de koop binnen 14 dagen en de informatieplicht voor de verkoper. »» Populairste onderwerpen Octrooien Software , Europa , Inventiviteit , Spoedcursus , FAQ (Engelstalig) Websites en blogs Legaal bloggen , Webdesign , Hyperlinks , MP3-sites , Zoekmachines Auteursrecht MP3 , File sharing , Foto's , Spoedcursus , Thuiskopie Beveiliging Virussen , Computercriminaliteit , E-mail , Wachtwoorden , Spyware Merken Spoedcursus (Engelstalig) , Domeinnamen , UDRP Internetten File sharing , Providers , Privacy , Op het werk , Meningsuiting Databankrecht Spoedcursus (Engelstalig) , In de VS (Engelstalig) , In Nederland Technologie File sharing , Encryptie , Digitale handtekeningen , TCP/IP (Engelstalig) Innovatie Uitvinden , Idee vastleggen , Open innovatie Software Open source , Licenties , Octrooien Intellectuele eigendom Wat is IE/IPR , Spoedcursussen (Engelstalig) Zakendoen Contracten , E-commerce , Elektronisch betalen , Wet Koop op Afstand Zie alle categorieën of alle artikelen . perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original agreement by removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to provide written or other records to prove prior possession of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that under the Dutch Trade Secrets Act, legitimately held information is required. The changed text replaces 'can show by its written or other records' with 'believes was in its possession', which introduces a subjective belief without requiring proof. It also removes that the information 'had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confidentiality' and that they are not breaching any obligations. This contradicts the principle of requiring legitimate prior possession and the protection of confidential information, as the Receiving Party can now claim prior possession without objective evidence. Although the scraped law snippets do not contain the precise law, the Law Explanation suggests a direct contradiction with the principles of the Dutch Trade Secrets Act regarding legitimate prior possession, making the contradiction clear and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes within 14 days of receipt of a wri tten request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have - 4 - been generated from any part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party undertakes within 14 days of receipt of a verbal request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party some documents and some other material in its possession, custody or control that contain some part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to destroy some documents and some other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have been generated from some part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces ambiguity regarding what is returned/destroyed. The terms 'some' and 'verbal request' creates uncertainty about the obligations, making the clause less enforceable and potentially conflicting with legal standards requiring clear contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 6:227 of the Dutch Civil Code (Burgerlijk Wetboek)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6:227 of the Dutch Civil Code requires that obligations in a contract be sufficiently clear and determinable. By changing the phrase to 'verbal request' and to return/destroy 'some', the agreement becomes too vague about the obligations, contradicting the requirement for certainty in contractual obligations under Dutch law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the return or destruction of 'all documents' containing confidential information upon a 'written request'. The changed text modifies this to 'some documents' upon a 'verbal request'. The Law Explanation states that Article 6:227 of the Dutch Civil Code requires contractual obligations to be 'sufficiently clear and determinable'. By changing 'all' to 'some' and 'written request' to 'verbal request', the contract introduces ambiguity, making the obligation unclear and potentially unenforceable. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which states that 'some' and 'verbal request' creates uncertainty, conflicting with legal standards requiring clear contractual obligations. The ambiguity directly contradicts the requirement for certainty, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of The Netherlands and the competent court in The Hague, The Netherlands shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of any country and the competent court in any location shall have jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.explanation: By stating 'any country' and 'any location', the governing law and jurisdiction become unclear. This can lead to forum shopping and undermines the principle of contractual certainty, and might render the jurisdiction clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 3 Rome Convention on the Law Applicable to Contractual Obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this is an EU law, The Netherlands is part of it, which suggests a specified choice of law and court. The clause 'any country' and 'any location' can lead to forum shopping, which conflicts with the principle of party autonomy which can undermine the ability to litigate in The Netherlands.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies The Netherlands as the governing law and The Hague as the jurisdiction. The changed text broadens this to 'any country' and 'any location,' which directly contradicts the principle of contractual certainty. The Law Explanation suggests this change leads to forum shopping, undermining the ability to litigate in The Netherlands as initially agreed. This constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the revised terms deviate significantly from the original specified jurisdiction, making enforcement ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The scraped snippets are not helpful.
